data.title: Communication Services biggest turkeys; worst performing stocks YTD
data.description:
Communication services (XLC) is the second top performing sector year-to-date, behind technology.

It has recovered nicely since the October lows and stands at around +18% year-to-date. It is also surpassing the performance of the S&P 500 (SP500), which is up 15% year-to-date.

In addition, the sector has shown strong momentum since the Federal Reserve’s first rate cut in September, up 10% since then.

Here are the worst performing U.S.-based large-cap stocks within communication services, year-to-date:

  1. The Trade Desk (TTD) -66.7% YTD; Seeking Alpha Quant rating: 2.62
  2. Charter Communications (CHTR) -41.4% YTD; Seeking Alpha Quant rating: 2.82
  3. Snap (SNAP) -29.1% YTD; Seeking Alpha Quant rating: 3.27
  4. Comcast Corp. (CMCSA) -28.9% YTD; Seeking Alpha Quant rating: 3.28
  5. Omnicom Group (OMC) -15.3% YTD; Seeking Alpha Quant rating: 3.05
  6. Pinterest (PINS) -12% YTD; Seeking Alpha Quant rating: 3.00
  7. Warner Music Group (WMG) -8.6% YTD; Seeking Alpha Quant rating: 2.76
  8. News Corp. (NWSA) -7.7% YTD; Seeking Alpha Quant rating: 2.69
  9. The Walt Disney Co. (DIS) -7.2% YTD; Seeking Alpha Quant rating: 3.39
  10. AT&T Inc. 5% DEP RP PFD A (T.PR.A) -4.9% YTD; Seeking Alpha Quant rating: NA

data.title: Wall Street rises on fresh economic signals ahead of Thanksgiving
data.description:
Wall Street watched stocks climb higher on Wednesday, with traders and investors digesting new economic data amid ongoing rate-cut speculation.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) which rallied north of 650 points on Tuesday was last higher by 0.8%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) pushed up by 0.8% and the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) advanced by 0.9%. 

Looking at the S&P 500 by sector, nine of the 11 segments are trending higher, with Info Tech leading the gains. On the downside, Communication Services has been the weakest performing area so far. 

Treasury yields advanced as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved higher by 1 basis point to 4.00% and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis points to 3.48%. 

On the economic front, initial jobless claims surprised lower in the week ended November 22. Durable goods orders slowed in September, as expected. 

Also, the Federal Reserve's Beige Book report highlighted that most of the Federal Reserve's 12 districts said U.S. economic activity was "little changed" in recent weeks.

“Stocks are celebrating the likelihood of a December rate cut, as it's becoming clear that the labor market is the priority for the Federal Reserve, and another rate cut would help to protect against any further cooling of the job market,” Paul Stanley, chief investment officer, Granite Bay Wealth Management stated. 

“It's becoming more clear that the stock market declines seen over most of November were simply a pullback after an extremely strong run in October and September,” Stanley added. 

As for stocks that are on the move, shares of Robinhood Markets (HOOD) tracked higher by 10.6% on the day while shares of Zscaler (ZS) dropped 12.4%.

For reference, markets will be closed on November 27, Thursday, for the Thanksgiving holiday.

More on markets

  • Thanksgiving feast of gains: 10 best S&P 500 stocks of 2025
  • Thanksgiving leftovers no one wants: 10 worst S&P 500 stocks of 2025
  • Black Friday, Cyber Monday, and 10 retail stocks to watch
  • Don't Buy This Dip: It's Black Friday In The Equity Market
  • AI Spending War And AI Debt Pile-Up Could Squeeze Share Buybacks

data.title: Deere signals $4B–$4.75B net income forecast for 2026 as it targets growth in small ag, turf, and construction
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Deere & Company (DE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • John May, Chairman, President & CEO, stated, "2025 marked a year of significant challenges and uncertainty, but it also reflected the resilience and strength of the Deere organization as we continue to demonstrate structurally higher performance levels while making substantial progress on our Smart Industrial journey." May highlighted delivering over $5 billion in net income and equipment operations OROS of 12.6%, including a 1.5 point impact from tariffs. He emphasized advancements in technology, notably the growth of the company's tech stack across all layers from base precision to full autonomy and digital engagement.
  • Christopher Seibert reported, "Net sales and revenues were up 11% to $12.4 billion, while net sales for the equipment operations were up 14% to $10.6 billion. Net income attributable to Deere & Company decreased to $1.1 billion or $3.93 per diluted share." He noted strong margin performance and ongoing diversification away from North American large ag, with growth visibility in small ag, turf, and construction forestry.
  • Josh Jepsen, Senior VP & CFO, commented, "Our performance yielded $18.50 in earnings per share that supported continued strong cash generation. Equipment operations cash flow was $5.1 billion, a significant improvement from past downturns and better than any year outside of the period of '21 to 2024, which enabled us to return over $2.8 billion to shareholders via dividends and share repurchases."
  • Deanna Kovar, President of Worldwide Ag & Turf Division, highlighted inventory reduction achievements and a focus on healthy product mix, stating confidence in used inventory momentum and cautious production to match demand.

Outlook

  • Management projects fiscal year 2026 net income in the range of $4 billion to $4.75 billion, including projected pretax direct tariff expense of approximately $1.2 billion.
  • Operating margin guidance for Production & Precision Ag is forecasted between 11% and 13%, reflecting stability in international markets amid continued headwinds in North American large ag and tariffs. Small Ag & Turf segment net sales are projected to be up around 10% with operating margin between 12.5% and 14%. Construction & Forestry net sales are expected to rise around 10%, with an operating margin between 8% and 10%.
  • The company expects industry sales of large equipment in the U.S. and Canada to be down 15% to 20%, while small ag and turf demand is estimated to be flat to up 5%.
  • For Q1 2026, Production & Precision Ag net sales are anticipated to be close to Q1 2025, but margins will be significantly lower due to lean production and product mix.

Financial Results

  • Fiscal year 2025 net sales and revenues declined 12% to $45.7 billion, and equipment operations net sales dropped 13% to $38.9 billion.
  • Q4 net sales and revenues were $12.4 billion, and net sales for equipment operations were $10.6 billion.
  • Q4 net income attributable to Deere & Company was $1.1 billion or $3.93 per diluted share.
  • Segment performance: Production & Precision Ag Q4 net sales of $4.74 billion, operating profit of $604 million, and a 12.7% margin; Small Ag & Turf Q4 net sales of $2.457 billion, operating profit of $25 million; Construction & Forestry Q4 net sales of $3.382 billion, operating profit of $348 million.
  • Financial Services Q4 net income was $293 million; fiscal 2026 forecast for this segment is $830 million.

Q&A

  • Stephen Volkmann, Jefferies: Asked about tariff offset strategy for the $1.2 billion headwind. Josh Beal responded, "Run rate of the tariffs by quarter is pretty evenly spread, roughly $300 million per quarter... we expect to be price/cost positive. So we'll start to capture back -- we'll capture the incremental exposure this year and some of the exposure that we saw in 2025."
  • Jamie Cook, Truist: Inquired about the high decremental margin in Production and Precision Ag. Beal stated, "Tariffs for that business have approximately 1.5 points or so margin impact for the full year of 2026... that delta between normal would be the geographic mix that you're seeing."
  • Kristen Owen, Oppenheimer: Queried about the 1.5% price assumption for large ag. Beal explained, "You're right, early order programs in large tractor pricing in North America for 2026, list price increases are in the range of 3% to 4%... what you're seeing with the 1.5% price guide is a couple of things... geographic mix... and a parts mix versus complete goods."
  • Tim Thein, Raymond James: Asked about production costs ex-tariffs for 2026. Beal replied, "Overheads for our business, particularly in large ag are expected to be unfavorable next year... we would still expect to be slightly unfavorable for production cost in 2026."
  • David Raso, Evercore: Questioned quarterly cadence for PP&A. Beal said, "We would expect to be down year-over-year in all those quarters, just given the level of decline that we're seeing. But you see margin improve quite a bit as you get into the second quarter."
  • Jerry Revich, Wells Fargo: Asked about See & Spray adoption. Beal noted, "Take rates of See & Spray on our 2026 early order program at a pretty similar level to what we saw in 2025."
  • Chad Dillard, Bernstein: Sought clarification on guidance assumptions for government assistance and operating leverage. Beal stated, "Our baseline expectation is not for any more assistance... we've intentionally put some flexibility in the back half to respond to changes in velocity."
  • Steven Fisher, UBS: Asked about South America confidence and turf segment housing link. Beal said, "A couple of things on South America, we've talked about kind of a mix of caution and optimism... On small ag, Steve, and specific to turf, our forecast is based off of housing starts coming up a little bit next year, not a massive increase."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on tariffs, decremental margins, price assumptions, and production costs, with a cautious to slightly negative tone, particularly on the impact of tariffs and North American ag demand. Questions frequently sought clarification and expressed concern about cost headwinds and demand uncertainty.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone in prepared remarks, with phrases such as "we expect to be price/cost positive" and "we have the right tools in place to sustain this positive momentum." During Q&A, management provided detailed explanations but acknowledged headwinds and uncertainty, using language like "we would still expect to be slightly unfavorable" and "the delta... would be the geographic mix."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone appeared more focused on near-term cost pressures, while management's tone remained confident in structural improvements and technology-driven growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for net income shifted lower for fiscal 2026 at $4 billion to $4.75 billion, compared to a previous range of $4.75 billion to $5.25 billion for fiscal 2025.
  • Operating margin guidance for Production & Precision Ag was reduced compared to the prior quarter, with a stated range of 11% to 13% versus prior guidance of 15.5% to 17%.
  • Management highlighted higher tariff headwinds for 2026, up to $1.2 billion from $600 million the previous year.
  • Analysts' questions this quarter concentrated more on tariff mitigation, decremental margins, and cost structure, while the previous quarter included more focus on inventory and demand inflection timing.
  • Management's tone regarding technology adoption and structural improvements remained positive, but the language around North American ag demand and tariffs signaled increased caution.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited persistently high tariffs, with a pretax direct tariff expense of $1.2 billion forecast for 2026.
  • North American large ag industry expected to decline another year, with industry sales down 15% to 20%.
  • High production costs, unfavorable overheads, and labor contract step-ups are anticipated headwinds.
  • Management emphasized ongoing actions to reduce used inventory, maintain lean production, and preserve flexibility to respond to market changes.

Final Takeaway

Deere’s leadership highlighted the company’s resilience through a challenging year, underlining ongoing cost discipline, lean inventory, and focused investment in digital and autonomous technologies. Management set expectations for lower net income in 2026, citing significant tariff and cost headwinds, while signaling growth in small ag, turf, and construction forestry segments. The company’s approach centers on cautious production, used inventory management, and leveraging technology to unlock future value, with flexibility built into plans to respond swiftly as market demand recovers.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. economy continues treading water, Fed's Beige Book report
data.description:
Most of the Federal Reserve's 12 districts said U.S. economic activity was "little changed" in recent weeks, according to the Federal Reserve's Beige Book report released on Wednesday, which provides anecdotal observations of the economy.

Two districts saw a modest decline, one saw modest growth, and the rest saw tepid activity. "Outlooks were largely unchanged, overall," the report said. However, some contacts reported an increased risk of slower activity in upcoming months, while some optimism was noted among manufacturers.

In the previous report, released in mid-October, U.S. economic activity "changed little" in the prior weeks.

Developing... check back for updates.

data.title: Pentagon said to have named Alibaba, Baidu, and BYD for inclusion in U.S. CMC list
data.description:
The names of Alibaba (BABA), Baidu (BIDU), and BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) were suggested by the Pentagon to be included in the list of Chinese military companies, Bloomberg reported on Wednesday, citing a letter addressing U.S. Congress.

The report said Deputy Defense Secretary Stephen Feinberg informed lawmakers of the Pentagon's stance on the Chinese companies in an Oct. 7 letter, which addressed the heads of the House and Senate Armed Services Committees.

It wasn’t clear whether the companies have been formally included in the Pentagon’s 1260H list, which serves as a major warning to U.S. investors on certain Chinese firms, the report said.

Feinberg said the three companies, along with five others—Eoptolink Technology Inc., Hua Hong Semiconductor Ltd., RoboSense Technology Co., WuXi AppTec Co., and Zhongji Innolight Co.—merit inclusion on the 1260H list.

"In our review of the latest information available, the Department has identified eight entities that it has determined are 'Chinese military companies' in accordance with the statute that should be added to the 1260H list," Feinberg wrote in the letter.

More on Chinese companies

  • Chinese automakers pressure the Japanese market with lower prices
  • China's BYD to phase out in-house D-Chain payments as supplier pressure mounts
  • Baidu shares jump after J.P. Morgan's bullish upgrade
  • Baidu's AI cloud ops continue to absorb ad biz weakness in Q3
  • Alibaba's Q3 report underscored by 'robust AI demand' in cloud unit

data.title: BBVA Argentina maintains 45–50% loan growth target for 2025 while navigating higher NPLs and rate volatility
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Banco BBVA Argentina S.A. (BBAR) Q3 2025

Management View

  • BBVA Argentina sustained its growth strategy in Q3 2025, supported by operational efficiency and strict expense control, despite a volatile interest rate environment and political uncertainty. María Belén Fourcade stated the Central Bank's tighter monetary policy and electoral uncertainty led to "a sharp rise in the level and volatility of interest rates," with deposit rates peaking at 70% in September.
  • The bank achieved a 6.7% real-term increase in total loans to the private sector and a consolidated market share of 11.39%. Deposits grew 10.2% in real terms, pushing market share to 10.09%. The liquidity ratio was 44.3%, and capital ratio stood at 16.7%, down 170 basis points from the previous quarter due to a temporary sovereign debt valuation impact.
  • Fourcade reported inflation-adjusted net income of ARS 38.1 billion for Q3 2025, down 39.7% quarter-over-quarter, with a quarterly ROE of 4.7% and ROA of 0.7%. The decline was attributed to "lower operating income, mainly due to: one, a deterioration in loan loss allowances; two, lower net interest income; and three, a drop in the line of net income from measurement of financial instruments at fair value through P&L."
  • Net interest income was ARS 585.5 billion, decreasing 6.6% quarter-over-quarter. Loan loss allowances rose 37.1%, with cost of risk at 6.63%. Net fee income surged 37.5% to ARS 137.1 billion due to pricing strategy alignment. Operating expenses fell 3.4% to ARS 494.6 billion.
  • Fourcade highlighted the bank's "notable resilience and effective management," citing positive credit growth, below-average delinquency, and robust liquidity and capital ratios.

Outlook

  • CFO Carmen Arroyo indicated guidance remains unchanged: "we are okay with this 45% to 50% in real terms loan growth. In terms of deposit, we see something more than what you said. So something similar to 30%, 35% could be okay for us. ROE, we are thinking of high single digits. And in terms of capital ratio, finishing the year near the 17%, around 17%." The guidance for 2026 is "mid- to low teens" ROE.
  • No explicit changes in guidance from previous quarter were announced. Arroyo emphasized, "there's no change in our guidance. We are maintaining a similar view for the whole year."

Financial Results

  • Inflation-adjusted net income for Q3 was ARS 38.1 billion, a decrease of 39.7% from the previous quarter.
  • Net interest income stood at ARS 585.5 billion, down 6.6% quarter-over-quarter. Loan loss allowances increased 37.1% to reflect higher nonperforming loans, particularly in retail.
  • Net fee income rose to ARS 137.1 billion, up 37.5% from Q2. Operating expenses were ARS 494.6 billion, decreasing 3.4%. Private sector loans totaled ARS 12.8 trillion, representing 6.7% real growth. Total deposits reached ARS 15.4 trillion, an 11.2% quarter-over-quarter increase.
  • The NPL ratio reached 3.28% for private loans, remaining below the system average. Liquidity ratio dropped to 44.3% from 48.7% in Q2, and capital ratio fell to 16.7% from 18.4% in Q2.

Q&A

  • Brian Flores, Citigroup: Asked if real loan growth and deposits guidance were maintained and about the genuineness of loan growth. Arroyo confirmed "45% to 50% in real terms loan growth" and "something similar to 30%, 35%" deposit growth, with ROE in high single digits and capital ratio near 17%. She clarified loan growth is "fully genuine," mainly driven by U.S. dollar loans and commercial lending, while personal loans were flat due to prudency amid rising NPLs.
  • Daer Labarta, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about reserve requirements, NIM outlook, and NPL trajectory. Diego Cesarini explained reserve requirement changes will "reduce this inefficiency to 0" and improve profitability, expecting NIMs to "see higher NIMs" in Q4 and stability into 2026. Arroyo projected NPLs "maybe we see something higher" in Q4, with improvement expected in 2026.
  • Carlos Gomez-Lopez, HSBC: Asked about loan demand, optimal capital levels, and risk-weighted asset changes. Arroyo anticipated slow recovery for retail credit demand and strong commercial loan demand, expecting 2025 to finish with a capital ratio "around 17%" and a management comfort level "a little below 13%." Cesarini clarified the Q3 capital drop was due to public sector debt valuation and was temporary.
  • Pedro Ofenhagenen, Latin Securities: Queried risks for credit growth in 2026. Arroyo said "no issue related to capital or liquidity," but emphasized caution on retail NPLs, expecting normalization after early 2026. Commercial segment shows strong demand.
  • Mateus Raffaelli, Itaú: Asked about coverage ratio trends. Arroyo stated the bank is "comfortable in this 98% to 100% levels for 2025," projecting improvement in 2026.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly cautious, focusing on sustainability of growth, NPLs, capital levels, and the impact of regulatory changes. Most questions probed the durability of guidance and asset quality amid a challenging macro environment.
  • Management maintained a confident yet measured tone, repeatedly emphasizing resilience, stability, and prudent risk management. Confidence was evident in statements such as "we are okay with this 45% to 50% in real terms loan growth" and "we are maintaining a similar view for the whole year."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence was steady, though more defensively framed due to higher NPLs and the sharp drop in net income. Analysts continued to press on asset quality and growth sustainability, but without notable escalation in skepticism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • No change in loan growth or deposit growth guidance; both quarters targeted 45–50% loan growth and 30–35% deposit growth.
  • Capital ratio declined to 16.7% from 18.4%, attributed to temporary sovereign debt valuation, but management expects recovery.
  • Asset quality worsened, with NPL ratio rising to 3.28% from 2.28% in Q2, and cost of risk increasing, reflecting macro headwinds.
  • Net income fell sharply from ARS 59.6 billion in Q2 to ARS 38.1 billion in Q3, with ROE and ROA also declining.
  • Management’s tone shifted from a focus on stabilization and growth to more caution regarding NPLs and market volatility.
  • Analysts' focus remained on growth sustainability, asset quality, and the impact of regulatory and market shifts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited rising NPLs in the retail portfolio, particularly credit cards and consumer loans, as a key concern, noting "the retail side regarding personal loans and credit cards is delicate due to NPL growth."
  • Regulatory changes, especially regarding reserve requirements and interest rates, increased funding costs and impacted intermediation margins.
  • Liquidity and capital ratios remain strong, with management not anticipating restrictions in these areas.
  • Macro and political volatility, including rapidly shifting interest rates and electoral uncertainty, were significant challenges during the quarter.

Final Takeaway

BBVA Argentina reported a challenging third quarter marked by market and political volatility, leading to higher NPLs and a decline in profitability. Despite these headwinds, management reiterated its commitment to delivering 45–50% loan growth and maintaining deposit growth in the 30–35% range for 2025, supported by prudent risk management and operational efficiency. The bank expects asset quality pressures to ease in 2026, with capital and liquidity levels remaining ample to support continued growth and shareholder value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Apple challenges India's antitrust penalty law with risk of up to $38B fine: report
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) is challenging India's new antitrust penalty law under which the company could potentially face a fine of up to $38B, Reuters reported, citing a court filing at the Delhi High Court.

The challenge is the first against India's antitrust penalty law that since last year, enables the Competition Commission of India, or CCI, to use global turnover when calculating fines it imposes on companies for abusing their market dominance, the report added.

Apple did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Since 2022, Tinder-owner Match (MTCH) and Indian startups have been in an antitrust tussle with Apple at the CCI, the report noted.

In July last year it was reported that a probe by the CCI found that Apple exploited its dominant position in the app store market on iOS, engaging "in abusive conduct and practices."

Apple denied all wrongdoing, and the CCI is yet to make a final decision in the case, including about any penalty, according to the report.

The U.S. tech giant is requesting judges to declare as illegal the 2024 law that allowed the CCI to use global turnover, not just that in India, when calculating fines, the report noted.

Apple's "maximum penalty exposure" at the rate of 10% of its average global turnover derived from all of its services globally for three fiscal years to 2024 could be about $38B, the report added.

Such a "penalty based on global turnover...would be manifestly arbitrary, unconstitutional, grossly disproportionate, unjust," the filing added.

Apple cited the CCI's use of the new rules for the first time on November 10 in an unrelated case, where they were retrospectively applied to a breach by the affected company a decade earlier, the report noted.

Apple has "no choice but to bring this constitutional challenge now to avoid retrospective imposition of penalty against them," the filing noted, as per the report.

Apple notes that it is a small player compared to Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) unit Google's Android, which is the dominant player in the Indian market.

Last year, the CCI found that Apple was not allowing any third-party payment processor to provide services for in-app purchases, where the fee could be up to 30%.

In a private submission to the CCI, reported by the news outlet in October, Apple's rival Match argued a fine based on global turnover could "act as a significant deterrent against recidivism," as per the report.

In its court filing, Apple noted that India should only impose a fine based on the Indian revenue of the specific unit that breaches antitrust law, giving an example of a toy seller running a stationery business, the report noted.

The company also noted in the filing that it would be arbitrary and disproportionate to impose a fine on the stationery business's total turnover of 20,000 rupees, when the contravention is only in relation to the toy business that earns 100 rupees.

Apple's plea will be heard on Dec. 3, the report added.

data.title: U.S. pending home sales dip in November amid financial uncertainty, Redfin says
data.description:
U.S. pending home sales fell 2.1%  Y/Y to 73,223 during the four weeks ended Nov. 23, the biggest decline in eight months, real estate brokerage Redfin said on Wednesday. The November dip followed a 0.3% Y/Y decline in the four weeks ended Nov. 9.

Home prices are increasing and mortgage rates ticked up last week after slipping to their lowest level in a year. "Rising costs and widespread financial uncertainty are causing house hunters to retreat," the company, which is owned by Rocket Companies (RKT) said.

New listings rose 2% Y/Y to 75,794, compared with a 3.4% increase in the four weeks ended Nov. 9. Active listings rose 5.7% to 1.17M, its smallest increase since February 2024 and down from a 6.3% rise in the prior report.

The median sale price rose 2.4% Y/Y to $393,248, the biggest increase in almost eight months, while the median asking price increased 2.5% to $386,973.

data.title: PENN inks extension with Kambi as it resets its long-term sportsbook plans
data.description:
PENN Entertainment (PENN) announced on Wednesday that it reached a deal with Kambi Group (KAMBI) (OTCPK:KMBIF) to extend the term of their retail sportsbook platform agreement to July 31, 2027, superseding the previous agreement set to expire on December 31. Kambi (KAMBI) (OTCPK:KMBIF) provides B2B sports betting technology and fully managed sportsbook services to licensed gambling operators globally, including software, odds, trading, and risk management.

The new agreement relates to on-property sportsbooks active by the end of 2025, with Kambi currently supporting 30 PENN (PENN) properties in 13 U.S. states. Under the terms of the extension, Kambi's (KAMBI) (OTCPK:KMBIF) premium retail sportsbook technology will continue to power PENN’s on-property sportsbooks, providing PENN with additional flexibility as it prepares for migration to its proprietary technology. Notably, PENN (PENN) is unwinding its ESPN BET partnership, pivoting its digital strategy toward an iCasino- and theScore Bet–led model.  "This agreement ensures PENN can continue to benefit from our high-performance technology while maintaining the flexibility to execute its long-term strategic plans," stated Kambi CEO Werner Becher. 

Kambi (KAMBI) (OTCPK:KMBIF) is primarily traded on the Nasdaq First North Growth Market in Stockholm. It is also available via secondary listings on venues such as the London Stock Exchange and over-the-counter markets.

Shares of PENN were up 1.7% in Wednesday afternoon trading. The gaming stock is up more than 7% over the last week.

data.title: Target sees $115M in costs related to corporate job cuts
data.description:
• In a  filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission on Wednesday, Target (TGT) confirmed that it reduced its corporate workforce as part of a multi-year initiative to transform various aspects of the business and optimize assets. Announced late October, this was the second largest workforce reduction in Target’s (TGT) corporate staff.
  • Costs incurred in connection with this action included severance and “related costs” of $115M within the company’s selling, general & administrative expenses during the third quarter, the majority of which are expected to be paid in Q4.
  • Target (TGT) also recognized $46M in impairments and other charges associated with the termination of a commercial partnership in Q3.
  • Target (TGT) shares are nearly 4% higher on Wednesday.
